Using rideshare apps, staying in tourist districts at night, and maintaining awareness in crowded spaces will ensure a trouble-free visit.

neighborhoods.name: Casco Viejo
neighborhoods.description: The historic district is heavily policed with tourist police presence day and night. While surrounded by poorer areas, the district itself is very secure for visitors.

neighborhoods.name: Punta Pacifica
neighborhoods.description: Modern financial district with excellent security, upscale hotels and restaurants. Well-lit and patrolled, making it one of the safest areas for tourists.

neighborhoods.name: El Cangrejo
neighborhoods.description: Central business district with good infrastructure and police presence. Popular with business travelers and generally safe during day and evening.

neighborhoods.name: Amador Causeway
neighborhoods.description: Tourist area with restaurants and attractions. Well-maintained with regular patrols, though exercise normal caution especially in the evening.

neighborhoods.name: San Miguelito
neighborhoods.description: High-crime district with the highest crime rate in the country. Tourists have no reason to visit and should avoid entirely, especially at night.

neighborhoods.name: El Chorrillo
neighborhoods.description: Despite being close to Casco Viejo, this impoverished area has high crime rates. The city has marked boundaries to advise tourists to stay out.

scams.name: The "Military Tourist" Scam
scams.description: A clean-cut American posing as military personnel approaches tourists with a story about being robbed and needing money for transport to Honduras or to replace ID. This scam has been reported for years in hotel bars and tourist areas.
scams.howToAvoid: Never give money to strangers with sob stories, regardless of how legitimate they appear. Real military personnel would contact their embassy, not approach tourists for money.

scams.name: Bird Poop/Distraction Scam
scams.description: Someone spills something on you (ketchup, water) and offers to help clean it up. While you're distracted, an accomplice steals your wallet or valuables.
scams.howToAvoid: Be suspicious of sudden spills or offers of help from strangers. Keep valuables secure and be aware of people around you in crowded areas.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Airport taxi drivers claim buses aren't running or Uber is illegal to charge inflated fares ($50+ when Uber costs $15-20). Some may also take longer routes to increase costs.
scams.howToAvoid: Use Uber or official rideshare apps when possible. If taking a taxi, negotiate the price before getting in and have small bills ready. Know approximate distances and costs.

scams.name: Fake Tour Operators
scams.description: Charming individuals approach tourists offering amazing tour deals at unbeatable prices, asking for upfront payment. They either disappear with the money or deliver a poor-quality experience.
scams.howToAvoid: Only book tours through reputable operators or your hotel. Never pay the full amount upfront to someone who approaches you on the street.

soloFemale.overview: Panama City is generally safe for solo female travelers who take standard precautions. Macho attitudes and catcalling are common but usually harmless. Tourist areas are well-policed and women regularly travel alone without incident. The main concerns are petty theft and unwanted attention rather than serious safety threats.
tips: Stay in well-lit tourist areas like Casco Viejo and Punta Pacifica, especially at night
Use Uber or official taxis rather than walking alone between neighborhoods
Dress modestly and keep shoulders covered when visiting official places or religious sites
Ignore catcalls and whistles - they're common but generally harmless
Carry minimal valuables and keep your bag in front of you in crowded areas

nightSafety.overview: Nighttime safety in Panama City varies dramatically by location. Tourist districts like Casco Viejo remain well-patrolled and safe after dark, while venturing into surrounding areas or walking between neighborhoods becomes risky. The golden rule is to use Uber at night rather than walking, even between seemingly safe areas.
tips: Use Uber or taxis for all nighttime transportation, even short distances
Stay within tourist zones like Casco Viejo, which has heavy police presence
Avoid walking alone on empty streets or through neighborhoods you don't know
Be cautious around Avenida Central and bus stations after dark
Keep nightlife activities within the main tourist and business districts

transport.metro: Panama City has Central America's only metro system, which is modern, clean, air-conditioned and very safe during the day. Costs only $0.35 per journey and connects major tourist areas including Casco Viejo and Albrook Terminal.
transport.rideshare: Uber is widely available, legal, safe and often cheaper than taxis. Highly recommended for tourists as it eliminates haggling and provides GPS tracking. DiDi and Cabify are also available alternatives.
transport.taxis: Yellow taxis are official but don't use meters. Negotiate price before getting in. Overcharging tourists is common, and some drivers may take longer routes. Use hotel taxis or official taxi stands when possible.
transport.tips: The golden rule is to use Uber, especially at night. Walking between neighborhoods, even safe ones, can lead through dangerous blocks. The metro is excellent for day travel and costs under $1.

customs: Greet people with handshakes in formal settings; light cheek kisses between women or men and women who are acquaintances
Use formal titles like Señor/Señora until invited to use first names, especially in business
Learn basic Spanish phrases as a sign of respect, though English is widely spoken in tourist areas
Dress modestly for official business - men should wear shirts and long pants, women should cover shoulders
Never put your purse on the floor in restaurants as it's considered bad luck for your money

health.overview: Panama City has good medical facilities with modern hospitals and English-speaking doctors. The main health risks are mosquito-borne diseases like dengue, chikungunya, and Zika. Malaria risk is minimal in the city itself.
health.water: Tap water in Panama City is generally safe to drink, but many tourists prefer bottled water to be cautious. Hotel and restaurant water is typically fine.
health.vaccinations: Routine vaccinations should be up-to-date. Hepatitis A and typhoid are recommended for most travelers. Yellow fever vaccination is required if arriving from countries with yellow fever risk, but not for Panama City itself.
health.altitude: Panama City is at sea level with a hot, humid tropical climate. Heat exhaustion and dehydration are risks. Hurricane season is June-November but direct hits are rare.

emergency.usEmbassy: U.S. Embassy Panama: +507 317-5000. Located at Avenida Demetrio Basilio Lakas, Building 783, Clayton. For emergencies involving U.S. citizens, contact +1-202-501-4444 from abroad or +1-888-407-4747 from U.S./Canada.

faq.q: Is Panama City safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Panama City is generally safe for tourists who stay aware of their surroundings and stick to tourist areas. Violent crime against tourists is rare, but petty theft is common in crowded areas.

faq.q: Is Panama City safe at night?
faq.a: Tourist areas like Casco Viejo are safe at night with heavy police presence, but avoid walking between neighborhoods. Use Uber for all nighttime transportation as even short walks can lead through dangerous areas.

faq.q: Is Panama City safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, solo female travelers generally feel safe in Panama City's tourist zones. Catcalling is common but usually harmless. Take standard precautions like staying in well-lit areas and using rideshare apps at night.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Panama City?
faq.a: Avoid San Miguelito (highest crime rate), El Chorrillo (despite being near Casco Viejo), and Curundu. Also avoid walking alone around Avenida Central and bus stations at night.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Panama City?
faq.a: The metro is very safe, modern and clean - highly recommended for day travel. Buses are cheaper but can be crowded with pickpockets. Uber is the safest option overall and often cheaper than taxis.

cities.name: Playa del Carmen
cities.summary: Playa del Carmen remains a popular and relatively safe Mexican Caribbean destination with low violent crime rates affecting tourists. While petty crime and tourist scams are common concerns, millions of visitors enjoy trouble-free vacations annually in this well-policed resort town.
cities.quickVerdict: Playa del Carmen is generally safe for tourists, particularly those staying in resort areas or central tourist zones like Fifth Avenue. The US State Department rates Mexico as Level 2 (Exercise Increased Caution), but Quintana Roo state enjoys enhanced security specifically to protect the crucial tourism industry. The main risks involve petty theft, taxi overcharging, and occasional police corruption rather than violent crime targeting tourists. Recent traveler safety data shows a score of 91 out of 100, indicating excellent visitor experiences with proper precautions.

neighborhoods.name: Fifth Avenue (Quinta Avenida)
neighborhoods.description: The main tourist drag with heavy foot traffic, police presence, and tourist police patrols. Well-lit and bustling but watch for pickpockets and vendors.

neighborhoods.name: Centro/Downtown Core
neighborhoods.description: Central area between the beach and highway with good security, hotels, and restaurants. Generally safe during day and evening hours.

neighborhoods.name: Playacar Resort Zone
neighborhoods.description: Upscale resort area with gated communities, private security, golf courses, and controlled access. Very safe for tourists and families.

neighborhoods.name: Beach Front Areas
neighborhoods.description: Popular beach zones are safe during daylight with lifeguards and vendors. Avoid isolated beach areas after 10 PM due to robbery risk.

neighborhoods.name: North of CTM Avenue
neighborhoods.description: Area beyond the main tourist zone with fewer visitors and reduced police presence. Exercise caution especially at night.

neighborhoods.name: Colosio Neighborhood
neighborhoods.description: Impoverished area north of 46th Street known for high crime rates and police calls. No tourist attractions; avoid this area entirely.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Drivers inflate prices for tourists, especially from airport or when intoxicated. Some may demand ATM withdrawals.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official taxi stands, negotiate fare upfront, or use WhatsApp taxi services. Know official zone rates.

scams.name: ATM Card Skimming
scams.description: Card skimmers placed on Fifth Avenue ATMs and tourist area machines to steal card information.
scams.howToAvoid: Only use ATMs inside banks with security cameras. Cover your PIN and inspect machines for tampering.

scams.name: Fake Hotel Worker Scam
scams.description: Street promoters claim to work at your hotel to gain trust and direct you to overpriced stores or timeshare presentations.
scams.howToAvoid: Verify credentials and ignore unsolicited approaches from street vendors claiming hotel affiliation.

scams.name: Police Extortion
scams.description: Corrupt officers may target tourists for fake drug searches or traffic violations to demand bribes.
scams.howToAvoid: Stay in well-lit tourist areas, avoid carrying large amounts of cash, and politely request official documentation.

soloFemale.overview: Playa del Carmen is generally safe for solo female travelers and ranks as the 7th safest place in Mexico for women traveling alone. The tourist areas have good security presence and many other solo female travelers visit regularly. However, standard precautions should be taken, especially regarding nightlife and transportation.
tips: Stay in central hotels near Fifth Avenue or Playacar for better security and lighting
Use WhatsApp taxi services or official taxi stands rather than hailing cabs on the street
Avoid walking alone on beaches after 10 PM and stick to well-lit main streets
Be cautious in nightlife areas and never leave drinks unattended due to spiking reports
Consider joining group activities or dance classes instead of clubbing alone

nightSafety.overview: Playa del Carmen's nightlife areas are generally safe with good tourist police presence, but risks increase after midnight, particularly on beaches and side streets. The main Fifth Avenue corridor remains busy and relatively secure throughout the night, though petty crime like pickpocketing becomes more common in crowded areas.
tips: Stay on well-lit main streets like Fifth Avenue and First Avenue near the beach
Avoid romantic walks on dark beach areas after 10 PM due to robbery incidents
Travel in groups when possible and use taxis for transportation after dark
Watch drinks carefully at beach clubs and bars due to reported spiking incidents
Keep valuables secured and avoid displaying expensive items or large amounts of cash

transport.metro: No metro system exists. Local transportation relies on buses, taxis, and colectivos (shared minivans).
transport.rideshare: Uber is not available. WhatsApp taxi services provide safer alternatives to street taxis with advance booking and known drivers.
transport.taxis: Official taxis are generally safe but overcharging tourists is common. Use taxi stands with posted rates or negotiate prices beforehand. Avoid unlicensed vehicles.
transport.tips: ADO buses connect safely to Cancun Airport and other destinations. Always agree on taxi fares upfront and consider using hotel transportation for airport transfers.

customs: Greet people with handshakes or cheek kisses; physical contact during greetings is customary and shows warmth
Use formal address (usted) with elders and new acquaintances; say 'con permiso' when passing by people
Tipping 10-15% is standard at restaurants; say 'buen provecho' when passing diners or starting meals

health.overview: Mexico requires no mandatory vaccinations for entry, but Hepatitis A, Typhoid, and routine vaccines are recommended. The region experiences mosquito-borne illnesses including dengue, chikungunya, and Zika at low levels.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Use bottled water for drinking and cooking. Most hotels and restaurants provide purified water.
health.vaccinations: Recommended: Hepatitis A, Typhoid, updated Tetanus. Consider Hepatitis B for adventure activities. Measles outbreaks reported in early 2026 with cases in Playa del Carmen.
health.altitude: Sea level location with tropical climate. Hurricane season runs June-November with peak August-October. Use mosquito repellent and sun protection.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Consular Agency Playa del Carmen: Address varies - contact main Mexico City embassy +52-55-5080-2000 for current local agent information. Canadian Consulate: +52-984-803-2411. For emergencies, contact your embassy through 911 system.

faq.q: Is Playa del Carmen safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Playa del Carmen is generally safe for tourists with millions visiting annually without incident. The main risks are petty crime and scams rather than violent crime. Stay in tourist areas and use common sense precautions.

faq.q: Is Playa del Carmen safe at night?
faq.a: The main tourist areas like Fifth Avenue are safe at night with good lighting and police presence. Avoid dark beach areas after 10 PM and use taxis for transportation. Stay in groups when possible and watch your drinks.

faq.q: Is Playa del Carmen safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, it ranks as one of Mexico's safest destinations for solo women. Many solo female travelers visit safely. Use official transportation, stay in well-lit tourist areas, and be cautious with nightlife activities.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Playa del Carmen?
faq.a: Avoid Colosio neighborhood north of 46th Street, dark beach areas after 10 PM, and areas beyond the main tourist zone between the highway and ocean. Stick to Fifth Avenue, downtown core, and Playacar areas.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Playa del Carmen?
faq.a: Local buses and colectivos are generally safe during daytime. Taxis are safe but may overcharge tourists - use official stands or WhatsApp taxi services. ADO buses for longer distances are reliable and secure.

cities.name: San José
cities.summary: San José is Costa Rica's bustling capital and main transit hub, currently experiencing increased crime levels despite the country's overall peaceful reputation. While violent crime targeting tourists is relatively uncommon, petty crime including theft and pickpocketing is a significant concern, particularly in downtown areas and on public transportation. The city maintains good infrastructure and healthcare services, but visitors should exercise heightened awareness, especially at night.
cities.quickVerdict: San José serves as most visitors' entry point to Costa Rica but presents moderate safety challenges as Central America's crime rates rise. The downtown core is generally safe during daylight hours but becomes riskier after dark, with petty crime being the primary concern for tourists. Most serious violent crimes are drug-related and don't typically target visitors. With proper precautions - avoiding flashy displays of wealth, staying in well-lit areas, and using official transportation - travelers can safely navigate the capital while accessing its cultural attractions and excellent healthcare facilities.

neighborhoods.name: Escazú
neighborhoods.description: Upscale suburb west of downtown favored by expats and wealthy locals, featuring modern shopping centers, international restaurants, and quality healthcare facilities like Hospital CIMA. Well-patrolled with good lighting and infrastructure.

neighborhoods.name: Rohrmoser
neighborhoods.description: Safe residential area near the airport popular with business travelers and diplomats. Features embassies, upscale hotels, and shopping areas with good security presence and lighting.

neighborhoods.name: Barrio Escalante
neighborhoods.description: Hip cultural district with art galleries, cafes, and restaurants. Generally safe during the day with active foot traffic, though caution needed at night on quieter side streets.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Core
neighborhoods.description: Main business and tourist area around Teatro Nacional and Central Market. Busy and relatively safe during daylight hours but becomes concerning after dark with increased petty crime risk.

neighborhoods.name: Coca-Cola Area
neighborhoods.description: Major bus terminal district with heavy transient traffic. High concentration of pickpockets, street vendors, and opportunistic crime. Exercise extreme caution with belongings and avoid lingering unnecessarily.

neighborhoods.name: Pavas
neighborhoods.description: Industrial and residential area west of downtown experiencing rising crime rates. Some safer pockets exist near shopping centers, but generally avoid walking alone, especially at night.

scams.name: Fake Taxi/Bus Information
scams.description: Airport taxi drivers or individuals near bus stations falsely claim buses aren't running, seats are sold out, or hotels are overbooked to sell expensive alternative transportation or accommodation.
scams.howToAvoid: Always verify information directly at official bus stations or hotel reception. Use only official airport taxis with proper markings and set rates.

scams.name: Currency Exchange Rate Manipulation
scams.description: Businesses use outdated exchange rates (around 500 colones per USD instead of current rates near 610+) when tourists pay in US dollars, pocketing the difference as extra profit.
scams.howToAvoid: Know the current exchange rate before traveling. Pay in local colones when possible or verify posted exchange rates at establishments.

scams.name: ATM Skimming and Surveillance
scams.description: Thieves install card readers on ATMs or watch for withdrawal amounts, then follow victims to rob them. Some criminals pose as helpful strangers offering assistance at machines.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks or busy shopping centers during daylight. Cover your PIN entry, check for unusual attachments, and vary your cash withdrawal locations.

scams.name: Friendly Stranger Approach
scams.description: English-speaking men approach tourists claiming to be from the US, strike up conversations, then invite victims for drinks or to explore areas where accomplices can rob or drug them.
scams.howToAvoid: Be polite but cautious with overly friendly strangers. Decline invitations to secondary locations and never leave drinks unattended.

soloFemale.overview: San José is moderately safe for solo female travelers with proper precautions, though it requires more vigilance than Costa Rica's tourist destinations. The city experiences typical urban safety challenges, with petty crime being the primary concern rather than gender-specific threats. Costa Rican culture is generally respectful toward women, and sexual harassment is less common than in some other Central American capitals. However, solo women should avoid walking alone after dark, particularly in downtown areas and around bus terminals.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in safer neighborhoods like Escazú or Rohrmoser rather than budget hostels in downtown areas
Use official red taxis or ride-share apps instead of walking after dark, and always verify driver identity before entering vehicles
Dress conservatively and avoid flashy jewelry or electronics that could attract unwanted attention from petty thieves
Keep emergency contacts readily available and inform someone of your daily plans when exploring the city alone

nightSafety.overview: San José becomes significantly less safe after dark, with most safety incidents involving tourists occurring during evening hours. The downtown core, while busy during the day, sees increased criminal activity at night as foot traffic decreases and lighting becomes inadequate in many areas. Even neighborhoods considered safe during daylight require caution after sunset, and walking alone is strongly discouraged anywhere in the city.
tips: Use official taxis or ride-share services for any nighttime movement rather than walking, even for short distances
Stick to well-lit main streets and avoid shortcuts through parks or quiet side streets after dark
If staying downtown, return to your accommodation by 8-9 PM and avoid venturing out again until morning
Keep nighttime activities to upscale areas like Escazú where security presence is stronger and lighting is better

transport.metro: San José lacks a metro system. Public buses are the main urban transport but can be crowded and targets for pickpocketing. Red Cross buses serve major routes and are generally safer than smaller operators.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates informally (technically illegal but widely used) and is often safer and more reliable than taxis. Drivers may ask you to sit in front to avoid police detection. Service is good in central areas but limited in outlying neighborhoods.
transport.taxis: Official red taxis with yellow triangles and meters are the safest option. Ensure meters are running and avoid unmarked vehicles. Orange taxis serve the airport with fixed rates. Tourist police recommend using hotel-arranged taxis when possible.
transport.tips: Take photos of taxi license plates before entering. Avoid buses after dark and keep belongings secure. For airport transfers, use official shuttle services or pre-arranged transportation rather than improvising upon arrival.

customs: Greet with firm handshakes and eye contact; close friends may exchange cheek kisses, especially women greeting anyone and men greeting women
Embrace 'Pura Vida' philosophy - meaning 'pure life' - used as greeting, goodbye, or expression of contentment, reflecting the laid-back Costa Rican lifestyle
Practice 'La Hora Tica' (Tico Time) - expect 15-30 minute delays for social events as punctuality is relaxed, though business meetings require timeliness
Dress neatly and conservatively, avoiding shorts and flip-flops in urban areas; Costa Ricans take pride in appearance even for casual occasions
Show respect for family values and Catholicism; avoid public displays of affection and dress modestly when visiting religious sites
Accept coffee graciously when offered as it's central to social interaction; refusing may be considered impolite in Costa Rican hospitality culture

health.overview: San José offers excellent healthcare by regional standards, with both public and private systems available. The city hosts some of Central America's best medical facilities, making it a medical tourism destination. Private hospitals provide care comparable to North American standards with English-speaking staff.
health.water: Tap water in San José is generally safe to drink and meets international standards. However, some travelers prefer bottled water during initial adjustment. Ice in restaurants and hotels is typically made from treated water and safe to consume.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required for San José beyond routine immunizations. Yellow fever vaccination needed only if arriving from endemic countries in South America or Africa. Consider Hepatitis A and Typhoid for extended stays or rural travel.
health.altitude: San José sits at 1,200 meters (3,937 feet) elevation in Costa Rica's Central Valley. The tropical highland climate features year-round temperatures of 15-26°C (59-79°F) with dry season December-April and rainy season May-November.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy San José: +506 2519-2000 (business hours), +506 8863-4895 (after hours emergencies). Located in Pavas area. For non-emergency services, visit during business hours Monday-Friday.

faq.q: Is San José safe for tourists?
faq.a: San José is moderately safe with proper precautions. Petty crime like pickpocketing is the main concern, especially downtown and around bus terminals. Violent crime against tourists is uncommon but increased caution is needed compared to Costa Rica's beach destinations.

faq.q: Is San José safe at night?
faq.a: San José becomes significantly less safe after dark. Downtown areas should be avoided at night, and walking alone is strongly discouraged anywhere in the city. Use official taxis or ride-share services for any nighttime movement.

faq.q: Is San José safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Solo female travelers can visit San José safely with heightened awareness. Stay in safer neighborhoods like Escazú, use official transportation, dress conservatively, and avoid walking alone after dark. Costa Rican culture is generally respectful toward women.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in San José?
faq.a: Avoid the Coca-Cola bus terminal area, downtown core after dark, and Pavas neighborhoods at night. Exercise extra caution around any bus stations, poorly lit side streets, and areas with heavy transient traffic.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in San José?
faq.a: Daytime bus travel is generally safe but watch for pickpockets. Avoid public buses at night. Official red taxis with meters are safer, and Uber (though technically illegal) is widely used and often more reliable than street taxis.

cities.name: San Juan
cities.summary: San Juan is generally safe for tourists, particularly in main areas like Old San Juan and Condado, with strong police presence and US-level healthcare infrastructure. While crime rates are higher than mainland US cities, most incidents are petty theft or drug-related violence concentrated in specific neighborhoods away from tourist zones.
cities.quickVerdict: San Juan offers a relatively safe Caribbean experience with the conveniences of US territory status. Tourist areas are well-policed and most visitors encounter no serious problems. However, exercise standard urban precautions as property crime occurs, and be aware that certain neighborhoods should be avoided. The city's LGBTQ-friendly atmosphere and familiar infrastructure make it accessible for diverse travelers.

neighborhoods.name: Old San Juan
neighborhoods.description: Historic walled city with strong police presence, well-lit streets, and constant foot traffic. Very walkable with numerous attractions, restaurants, and shops. Generally safe day and night due to heavy tourism and security.

neighborhoods.name: Condado
neighborhoods.description: Upscale beachfront district with luxury hotels, restaurants, and shopping. Good lighting and regular patrols. Popular with tourists and locals. Exercise normal precautions but generally secure area.

neighborhoods.name: Ocean Park
neighborhoods.description: Trendy residential area near beaches with boutique hotels and cafes. Quieter than Condado but well-maintained. Safe for walking during day, use caution at night in less populated areas.

neighborhoods.name: Isla Verde
neighborhoods.description: Beach area near airport with resorts and restaurants. Generally safe tourist zone but can feel isolated at night. Stay near main hotel areas and avoid empty beach sections after dark.

neighborhoods.name: Santurce
neighborhoods.description: Arts district with galleries, markets, and nightlife. Mixed area with both trendy spots and rougher sections. Safe during day in main areas, exercise increased caution at night and know your exact destination.

neighborhoods.name: La Perla
neighborhoods.description: Historic but impoverished neighborhood adjacent to Old San Juan. High crime rates and drug activity. Strongly advised to avoid despite its colorful appearance and fame from music videos. Visit only with organized tours if at all.

scams.name: Beach Theft
scams.description: Thieves target unattended belongings while tourists swim or sunbathe. Items stolen from bags, chairs, or beach towels when tourists are distracted.
scams.howToAvoid: Never leave valuables unattended. Use waterproof pouches for essentials. Designate someone to watch belongings or use hotel safes instead of bringing valuables to beach.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Drivers may not use meters, take longer routes, or claim no change for large bills. Some may quote inflated fixed prices to tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Use Uber or Lyft when possible. For regular taxis, insist meter is used or agree on fare beforehand. Have small bills ready and research typical rates.

scams.name: Rental Car Break-ins
scams.description: Thieves target rental cars with visible items inside, especially in parking lots near tourist attractions. GPS units and bags are common targets.
scams.howToAvoid: Keep nothing visible in car. Use paid, supervised parking when possible. Don't leave GPS units or charging cables visible. Park in well-lit, busy areas.

scams.name: Overpriced Tours
scams.description: Street promoters offer bio-bay tours or island excursions at inflated prices, sometimes with poor quality service or hidden fees.
scams.howToAvoid: Book tours through reputable companies or hotels. Compare prices online beforehand. Be wary of high-pressure sales tactics from street promoters.

soloFemale.overview: San Juan is one of the safer Caribbean destinations for solo female travelers, with many women reporting positive experiences. The city's US territory status provides familiar infrastructure and legal protections. Tourist areas like Old San Juan and Condado have good lighting and police presence. However, standard precautions are still necessary, especially regarding nightlife and transportation.
tips: Return to accommodation by sunset or stick to well-lit, busy areas after dark
Use Uber or reputable taxis rather than walking alone at night
Limit alcohol consumption to maintain awareness of surroundings
Share location with someone and avoid posting real-time locations on social media
Stay in main tourist areas and avoid neighborhoods like La Perla

nightSafety.overview: Nightlife in San Juan is generally safe in tourist zones with good police presence and lighting. Old San Juan and Condado have vibrant night scenes with restaurants, bars, and clubs operating safely. However, crime rates increase after dark, particularly muggings and theft. Some areas become unsafe at night and should be avoided entirely.
tips: Stick to busy, well-lit areas like Old San Juan's main streets and Condado hotel zones
Travel in groups when possible, especially when bar-hopping
Use Uber, Lyft, or hotel-arranged transportation rather than walking long distances
Avoid La Perla, isolated beach areas, and poorly lit residential streets
Keep valuables minimal and stay alert to your surroundings

transport.metro: San Juan has the Tren Urbano metro system connecting some areas, but coverage is limited. Generally safe and clean but doesn't serve major tourist areas directly.
transport.rideshare: Uber and Lyft operate throughout San Juan and are widely recommended. Reliable, safe, and convenient for tourists. Available 24/7 in main areas with reasonable wait times.
transport.taxis: Licensed taxis are white with roof lights and generally safe. Some drivers may not use meters or may overcharge tourists. Agree on fare beforehand or insist on meter usage.
transport.tips: Use rideshare apps when possible for transparency and convenience. Public buses (AMA) exist but can be unreliable. Rental cars are good for exploring outside San Juan but parking can be challenging in Old San Juan. Walking is excellent in Old San Juan and Condado during daylight hours.

customs: Dress modestly when visiting churches and avoid beachwear in town areas
Tip 15-20% at restaurants and bars, as this is expected and often a main source of income for service workers
Learning basic Spanish phrases is appreciated, though English is widely understood in tourist areas
Be respectful of local environment, especially protected areas like El Yunque rainforest
Puerto Ricans value politeness and formal greetings when meeting people for the first time

health.overview: San Juan has US-level healthcare with modern hospitals and clinics. Most US health insurance plans work in Puerto Rico. The healthcare system is among the best in the Caribbean region.
health.water: Tap water is safe to drink throughout San Juan as it meets US standards. Bottled water is widely available if preferred.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required beyond standard US recommendations. Routine vaccines should be up to date. Consider hepatitis A/B for some travelers.
health.altitude: San Juan is at sea level with tropical climate year-round. Hurricane season runs June-November with August-October being peak risk period. Dengue and Zika are present but risk is low with proper mosquito precautions.

emergency.usEmbassy: No US Embassy needed as Puerto Rico is US territory. For federal assistance: 1-888-407-4747

faq.q: Is San Juan safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, San Juan is generally safe for tourists, especially in main areas like Old San Juan, Condado, and Isla Verde. These areas have strong police presence and are well-lit. However, exercise normal urban precautions and avoid certain neighborhoods like La Perla.

faq.q: Is San Juan safe at night?
faq.a: Tourist areas like Old San Juan and Condado are relatively safe at night with good lighting and police presence. However, crime rates do increase after dark, so stick to busy areas, travel in groups when possible, and use Uber or taxis for transportation.

faq.q: Is San Juan safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: San Juan is one of the safer Caribbean destinations for solo female travelers. Many women visit safely, especially in tourist zones. Use standard precautions like limiting alcohol, avoiding isolated areas at night, and using rideshare for transportation.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in San Juan?
faq.a: Avoid La Perla neighborhood despite its colorful appearance, as it has high crime rates. Also avoid poorly lit residential areas at night, isolated beach sections after dark, and areas away from main tourist zones like parts of Santurce late at night.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in San Juan?
faq.a: The Tren Urbano metro is safe but has limited coverage. Uber and Lyft are widely recommended and very reliable. Regular taxis are generally safe but may overcharge tourists. Public buses exist but can be unreliable for tourists.

cities.name: Santo Domingo
cities.summary: Santo Domingo is generally safe for tourists who take standard precautions, though it has the highest crime rates in the Dominican Republic. The US State Department maintains a Level 2 advisory for increased caution.
cities.quickVerdict: Santo Domingo can be safely visited with proper precautions and awareness. While crime exists, especially petty theft and some violent crime, millions of tourists visit annually without incidents. The Zona Colonial (Colonial Zone) is well-patrolled by Tourist Police during the day but requires caution at night. Most crime affecting tourists involves pickpocketing, bag snatching, and occasional scams rather than serious violent crime. Using common sense, staying in tourist areas, and avoiding displays of wealth significantly reduce risks.

neighborhoods.name: Zona Colonial
neighborhoods.description: Historic center with UNESCO World Heritage status. Heavy tourist police presence during the day, vibrant restaurants and attractions. Becomes less safe after 6pm when it empties out.

neighborhoods.name: Piantini
neighborhoods.description: Upscale business district with good security, international restaurants, and modern amenities. One of the safest areas in the city with reliable infrastructure.

neighborhoods.name: Gazcue
neighborhoods.description: Middle-class residential area with embassies and museums. Generally safe during the day but requires caution on side streets and after dark.

neighborhoods.name: Cristo Rey
neighborhoods.description: Working-class neighborhood with higher crime rates. Frequent reports of robberies and gang activity. Tourists should avoid this area, especially at night.

neighborhoods.name: Villa Mella
neighborhoods.description: Northeastern district with higher poverty and crime rates. Limited police presence and frequent reports of theft. Not recommended for tourists.

neighborhoods.name: Arroyo Hondo
neighborhoods.description: Area with mixed residential and commercial zones. Higher crime rates including muggings and theft, particularly problematic after dark.

scams.name: Taxi Fare Manipulation
scams.description: Taxis don't use meters and drivers may overcharge tourists, especially from airports. Some unmarked taxis may be involved in robbery schemes.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official taxi companies like Apolo Taxi or Aero Taxi, confirm fares before starting, or use Uber which has fixed pricing.

scams.name: Credit Card and ATM Skimming
scams.description: Card skimmers at ATMs and payment terminals can steal financial information. Fake ATMs or compromised card readers target tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks or hotels, cover your PIN, check for unusual devices on card readers, and monitor your accounts regularly.

scams.name: Romance and Dating App Scams
scams.description: Criminals use dating apps to lure tourists to isolated locations for robbery. Victims are often taken to bars where they're drugged or overcharged.
scams.howToAvoid: Meet dates in public places, don't go to secluded locations, never leave drinks unattended, and be wary of someone pushing you to specific venues.

scams.name: Fake Police and Officials
scams.description: Criminals impersonate police officers or lawyers to extort money from tourists, often claiming document problems or traffic violations.
scams.howToAvoid: Ask for proper identification, know that real police won't demand immediate cash payments, and contact the embassy if you're suspicious of official requests.

soloFemale.overview: Solo female travelers can visit Santo Domingo safely with proper precautions, though extra vigilance is required. The city has a moderate level of safety for women, with catcalling being extremely common and constant. Tourist areas like Zona Colonial are generally safe during the day with police presence, but the city empties out after 6pm requiring careful planning for evening activities.
tips: Dress conservatively despite the heat as revealing clothing attracts unwanted attention
Use Uber or hotel-arranged taxis instead of street taxis, especially at night
Stay in well-lit, populated areas and avoid walking alone after dark
Don't display valuable jewelry, phones, or bags that could attract thieves
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from uncomfortable situations immediately

nightSafety.overview: Santo Domingo becomes significantly less safe after dark, particularly outside tourist zones. The Zona Colonial empties out around 6pm and should be avoided for walking at night. While some areas like Piantini remain relatively safe with good lighting and security, most neighborhoods become more dangerous after sunset with increased crime risk.
tips: Avoid walking alone at night, especially in poorly lit areas or outside tourist zones
Use registered taxis, Uber, or hotel transportation instead of walking at night
Stick to well-frequented restaurants and bars in safe areas like Piantini for nightlife
Don't get extremely intoxicated as it makes you vulnerable to crime
If staying out late, arrange transportation in advance rather than looking for it on the street

transport.metro: Santo Domingo Metro is highly rated for safety, modern, well-maintained, and typically crime-free. It's clean, air-conditioned, affordable at around $0.50 per ride, and operates daily from 6am-10:30pm. The system doesn't cover all tourist areas but is excellent for longer trips between central districts.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates throughout Santo Domingo and is generally safer and more reliable than traditional taxis. It offers fixed app-calculated fares, GPS tracking for security, and eliminates language barriers and fare negotiations. The service is available 24/7 and cars are usually in better condition with working seatbelts and air-conditioning.
transport.taxis: Traditional taxis don't use meters and require fare negotiation before starting your trip. Use reputable companies like Apolo Taxi (popular) or Aero Taxi rather than hailing cabs on the street. Confirm car color and estimated wait time when calling. Cash payment in Dominican Pesos is typically required.
transport.tips: Public transport poses some theft risk, especially at night. The metro is the safest public transport option. Avoid public buses (guaguas) which can be overcrowded and less secure. For safety, use hotel-recommended taxis or ride-share apps.

customs: Greet people with 'Buenos días/tardes/noches' when entering shops or restaurants - politeness is highly valued
Use formal titles like 'Señor' or 'Señora' when addressing older adults or in formal settings
Dress conservatively and neatly in public spaces - appearance matters in Dominican culture and reflects respect
Expect flexible timing for social events ('Dominican time') but arrive punctually for business meetings
Show respect for elders and family hierarchy - offer seats to older people and use polite language
Accept offered food or drinks graciously as refusing without explanation may be seen as rude

health.overview: Healthcare quality varies significantly between private and public facilities. Private hospitals in Santo Domingo like Plaza de la Salud General Hospital and Cedimat Medical Center offer high-quality care with English-speaking staff. Public facilities are well below First World standards and often lack supplies.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Use bottled or purified water for drinking, brushing teeth, and ice. Most hotels and restaurants provide safe bottled water. Sealed bottles are widely available throughout the city.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required from the US. Routine vaccines should be up to date including COVID-19, flu, hepatitis A. Yellow fever vaccination required if arriving from high-risk countries like Brazil.
health.altitude: Santo Domingo is at sea level with tropical maritime climate. High temperatures and humidity year-round with little seasonal variation. Average temperatures 77-86°F (25-30°C). Hurricane season runs June-November with heaviest rains May-November.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Santo Domingo: 809-567-7775, located at Av. República de Colombia #57. For after-hours emergencies contact 809-567-7775.

faq.q: Is Santo Domingo safe for tourists?
faq.a: Santo Domingo is moderately safe for tourists who take standard precautions. While it has the highest crime rate in the Dominican Republic, millions visit annually without incidents. Stay in tourist areas, use reliable transport, avoid displaying valuables, and exercise extra caution at night.

faq.q: Is Santo Domingo safe at night?
faq.a: Santo Domingo becomes less safe after dark, especially outside tourist zones. The Zona Colonial empties out around 6pm and walking should be avoided. Use taxis or Uber for nighttime transportation and stick to well-lit, populated areas like Piantini for evening activities.

faq.q: Is Santo Domingo safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Solo female travelers can visit safely with extra precautions. Catcalling is extremely common, so dress conservatively and stay alert. Use Uber instead of street taxis, avoid walking alone at night, and trust your instincts about removing yourself from uncomfortable situations.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Santo Domingo?
faq.a: Avoid Cristo Rey, Villa Mella, Arroyo Hondo, Los Prados, and San Carlos, especially at night. These areas have higher crime rates including robbery and gang activity. Even 'safer' areas require caution after dark when the city empties out.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Santo Domingo?
faq.a: The metro system is very safe, modern, and clean - it's the best public transport option. Avoid public buses (guaguas) which pose theft risks. Uber is safer than traditional taxis. If using taxis, call reputable companies like Apolo Taxi rather than hailing on the street.

cities.name: Bali
cities.summary: Bali is considered one of the safest destinations in Southeast Asia for tourists, with low violent crime rates and a welcoming local culture rooted in Hindu traditions. The main safety concerns are petty crime like phone snatching, traffic accidents involving scooters, and occasional natural disasters due to its location on the Pacific Ring of Fire.
cities.quickVerdict: Bali is generally safe for tourists when common-sense precautions are taken. The island has extremely low violent crime rates and a tourist-friendly infrastructure, but visitors should be aware of petty theft, especially motorbike snatching in tourist areas, and exercise caution on the roads. Natural disasters like earthquakes and volcanic activity are monitored, and health risks like Bali Belly are manageable with proper food and water safety. Solo female travelers report feeling comfortable and welcomed, making Bali an excellent choice for all types of travelers.

neighborhoods.name: Ubud
neighborhoods.description: The cultural heart of Bali with low crime rates, peaceful atmosphere, and strong community values. Very safe for solo travelers and families, with the main risks being traffic and wildlife encounters at temple areas.

neighborhoods.name: Sanur
neighborhoods.description: A family-friendly coastal town with calm beaches, slower pace, and minimal nightlife issues. Features a safe 7km beachfront promenade and is considered ideal for first-time visitors to Bali.

neighborhoods.name: Nusa Dua
neighborhoods.description: An upscale resort area with controlled access, private security, and minimal crime. The planned development offers excellent safety for families and luxury travelers.

neighborhoods.name: Seminyak
neighborhoods.description: Popular beach destination with higher tourist density and prices. Generally safe but requires more awareness due to busy nightlife scene and occasional petty crime targeting tourists.

neighborhoods.name: Canggu
neighborhoods.description: Hip surfer town with heavy traffic congestion and moderate crime rates. Digital nomad hotspot that's generally safe but requires caution due to chaotic roads and crowded party scenes.

neighborhoods.name: Kuta
neighborhoods.description: Budget party destination with the highest petty crime rates on the island. Popular with backpackers but requires extra vigilance, especially at night, due to aggressive vendors, scams, and alcohol-fueled incidents.

scams.name: Motorbike Phone Snatching
scams.description: Thieves on motorbikes grab phones and bags from tourists walking along roads or riding scooters. This is the most common crime affecting visitors in Bali.
scams.howToAvoid: Keep phones in secure bags, avoid holding them while walking near roads, use crossbody bags, and be extra cautious in busy tourist areas like Kuta and Seminyak.

scams.name: Fake Taxi Drivers
scams.description: Drivers claiming broken meters and charging inflated fixed prices, or fake Blue Bird taxis painted to deceive tourists. Some demand payment upfront or take detours to commission-paying shops.
scams.howToAvoid: Use Grab or Gojek ride-hailing apps, only use legitimate Blue Bird taxis with working meters, or book taxis through your hotel. Always verify the driver matches the app photo.

scams.name: Money Changer Sleight of Hand
scams.description: Unlicensed money changers offering attractive rates but using quick hand tricks to shortchange customers or replacing bills with counterfeits during counting.
scams.howToAvoid: Only use authorized money changers with licenses displayed, count money yourself before leaving, use banks or hotel exchanges, and avoid street-side kiosks offering too-good-to-be-true rates.

scams.name: Temple Donation Scams
scams.description: Fake officials approaching tourists before temple entrances demanding mandatory donations, showing guestbooks with inflated amounts from previous visitors to pressure payment.
scams.howToAvoid: Only pay at official ticket counters with printed receipts, ignore people demanding donations before the entrance gate, and remember that temple donations are always optional.

soloFemale.overview: Bali is considered one of the safest destinations in Asia for solo female travelers, with low rates of street harassment and a welcoming local culture. The island has excellent infrastructure for independent travel, with many other solo female travelers, digital nomad communities, and tourist-friendly services. Most women report feeling comfortable walking alone during the day and evening in main areas.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in safe areas like Ubud, Sanur, or central Seminyak
Use Grab or Gojek for transport rather than flagging down taxis, especially at night
Be extra cautious with drinks in nightlife areas - never leave them unattended or accept from strangers
Dress modestly when visiting temples and local communities - cover shoulders and knees
Keep valuables secure and use hotel safes - petty theft is the main concern for all travelers

nightSafety.overview: Bali nightlife is generally safe compared to many global destinations, with low violent crime rates and well-established tourist areas. The main risks involve drink spiking, petty theft in crowded venues, and transport safety after drinking. Areas like Ubud and Uluwatu are very safe at night, while party zones like Kuta require more caution.
tips: Never leave drinks unattended and watch them being prepared at bars and clubs
Use licensed transport like Grab or hotel-arranged rides rather than street taxis when drinking
Stay in groups when possible and avoid walking alone in poorly lit areas
Stick to reputable venues and avoid cheap alcohol deals which may contain methanol
Keep emergency contacts saved and share location with friends when going out

transport.metro: Bali has no metro system. The island relies on roads, scooters, taxis, and ride-hailing apps for transportation.
transport.rideshare: Grab and Gojek are the dominant ride-hailing apps, offering both car and motorcycle taxi services. They're considered safe, track all rides, and show upfront pricing. Both require local SIM cards for registration.
transport.taxis: Blue Bird is the most reputable taxi company with metered fares, but many fake Blue Bird taxis operate. Other taxis often overcharge tourists and refuse to use meters, especially near airports and tourist areas.
transport.tips: Download both Grab and Gojek apps before arrival. Avoid renting scooters unless you're experienced - traffic is chaotic and accidents are common. For airport transfers, book private transport in advance to avoid taxi mafia inflated prices.

customs: Use your right hand for giving, receiving items, and eating - the left hand is considered impure
Remove shoes before entering homes, temples, and some shops - look for cues from others
Dress modestly with covered shoulders and knees when visiting temples, wear provided sarongs
Never touch someone's head, especially children - the head is considered sacred in Hindu culture
Show respect during religious ceremonies and processions - observe quietly and don't interrupt

health.overview: Bali has good private healthcare facilities in tourist areas, with international-standard hospitals like BIMC and Siloam. Common health issues include Bali Belly (traveler's diarrhea), dengue fever during rainy season, and sun-related problems. Medical costs can be expensive without insurance.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink and even locals avoid it. Use only bottled or filtered water for drinking and brushing teeth. Ice at reputable restaurants is generally safe as it's made from filtered water.
health.vaccinations: No mandatory vaccinations required for entry. Consider hepatitis A and B, typhoid, and Japanese encephalitis depending on activities. Dengue vaccination is available locally for ages 6-45. Malaria risk is minimal in main tourist areas.
health.altitude: Bali is at sea level with a tropical climate. Main health risks are heat exhaustion, dehydration, and sun exposure. The island has high humidity and temperatures ranging from 24-33°C year-round.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Consulate General Surabaya covers Bali: +62 31 297 4000. For emergencies, call US Citizens Services at +62 31 297 4000 or after hours +1 202 501 4444.

faq.q: Is Bali safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Bali is considered one of the safest destinations in Southeast Asia with very low violent crime rates and a welcoming culture. The main concerns are petty theft, traffic accidents, and occasional natural disasters, all of which are manageable with basic precautions.

faq.q: Is Bali safe at night?
faq.a: Generally yes, especially in areas like Ubud and Sanur. Nightlife areas like Seminyak are safe but require normal caution. Kuta's party scene needs extra awareness. The main risks are drink spiking and petty theft, not violent crime.

faq.q: Is Bali safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, Bali is excellent for solo female travel with low harassment rates and a strong community of other solo travelers. The island is very welcoming to women traveling alone, with good infrastructure and safety measures in tourist areas.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Bali?
faq.a: No areas are completely dangerous, but exercise more caution in Kuta at night due to higher petty crime and party atmosphere. Remote northern areas have limited emergency services. Avoid unlicensed transport and isolated beaches after dark.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Bali?
faq.a: There's no public transport system - use Grab/Gojek ride-hailing apps which are safe and tracked. Avoid unofficial taxis and be very careful with scooters due to chaotic traffic. Blue Bird taxis are legitimate but expensive.

cities.name: Da Nang
cities.summary: Da Nang is widely considered one of Vietnam's safest major cities with low crime rates, friendly locals, and excellent tourist infrastructure. The city has a visible police presence, well-lit streets, and organized traffic compared to other Vietnamese cities. Most visitors enjoy trouble-free stays with only minor concerns about typhoons during the seasonal period.
cities.quickVerdict: Da Nang stands out as one of Southeast Asia's safest destinations for tourists and expats alike. With violent crime being extremely rare and petty theft uncommon, the city offers a secure environment for all types of travelers. The US State Department maintains a Level 1 advisory for Vietnam, recommending normal precautions. While the city faces occasional typhoons and minor tourist scams, these risks are manageable with basic awareness. The local government prioritizes tourism security, maintaining regular police patrols and tourist assistance programs.

neighborhoods.name: My Khe Beach
neighborhoods.description: The most popular and safest tourist area with upscale resorts, security guards, CCTV systems, and regular lifeguard presence. Well-lit at night with plenty of restaurants and bars.

neighborhoods.name: An Thuong
neighborhoods.description: Trendy beachside district with vibrant nightlife, cafes, and tourist amenities. Heavy foot traffic and police presence make it ideal for solo female travelers and nighttime activities.

neighborhoods.name: Hai Chau District (Downtown)
neighborhoods.description: The city center with cultural landmarks, markets, and business district. Well-populated with good lighting and frequent police patrols, though can be busy during peak hours.

neighborhoods.name: Son Tra Peninsula
neighborhoods.description: Scenic mountainous area with temples and nature trails. Generally safe during daylight hours but requires caution on winding roads and when hiking alone.

neighborhoods.name: Thanh Khe District
neighborhoods.description: Local residential area with authentic Vietnamese atmosphere. Safe for experienced travelers but may have language barriers and fewer tourist services.

neighborhoods.name: Cam Le District
neighborhoods.description: Quieter suburban area primarily for locals. While not unsafe, it's less developed for tourists with limited English signage and fewer amenities.

scams.name: Airport Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Unmetered taxi drivers at Da Nang Airport quote inflated prices (up to 10x normal rates) and may show fake Grab prices on their phones to justify high fares.
scams.howToAvoid: Use the official Grab app to check real prices, insist on metered taxis from licensed companies like Mai Linh or Vinasun, or book through your hotel.

scams.name: Motorbike Rental Scams
scams.description: Rental shops may claim fake damage to bikes, hold passports hostage for payment, or even steal back their own motorbikes to force customers to pay again.
scams.howToAvoid: Take detailed photos/videos before renting, never leave your passport as collateral (offer cash deposit), and choose reputable shops recommended by other travelers.

scams.name: Currency Exchange Manipulation
scams.description: Informal money changers offer poor exchange rates or give counterfeit/outdated bills while claiming 'no commission' to attract tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official banks like Vietcombank or BIDV for currency exchange, check current rates online beforehand, and inspect all bills carefully.

scams.name: Fake Tour Agencies
scams.description: Unlicensed operators offer cheap tours with poor service quality, fake itineraries, or hidden fees that surface during the trip.
scams.howToAvoid: Book through licensed agencies with good online reviews, verify credentials, and avoid deals that seem too good to be true.

soloFemale.overview: Da Nang is considered one of the safest cities in Vietnam for solo female travelers, ranking among the top destinations in Southeast Asia. Women report feeling comfortable walking alone during the day and in well-lit tourist areas at night. The city has low harassment rates, respectful local culture, and strong security presence in tourist zones.
tips: Avoid dark alleyways and isolated areas late at night; stick to main roads and well-lit neighborhoods
Use ride-hailing apps like Grab instead of flagging down street taxis for safer transportation
Choose accommodation in tourist-friendly areas like My Khe Beach or An Thuong with good lighting and 24/7 reception
Dress modestly when visiting temples and local areas; cover shoulders and knees to show respect
Share your location with trusted contacts when exploring remote areas or beaches alone

nightSafety.overview: Da Nang maintains good safety standards at night, particularly around popular areas like the Han River, My Khe Beach, and downtown districts. The city has well-lit streets, visible police patrols, and a thriving nightlife scene that operates safely until late hours. However, some residential areas may have poor lighting.
tips: Stay in well-populated tourist areas with good lighting such as An Thuong and the riverside district
Use reputable transportation like Grab or licensed taxis rather than walking alone in quiet areas
Choose busy restaurants and bars for evening activities; avoid secluded venues
Keep valuables secure and avoid displaying expensive items in crowded nightlife areas
Be aware that clubs typically close around 2 AM; plan transportation in advance

transport.metro: Da Nang does not have a metro system. Public buses are available but have limited English signage and can be overcrowded.
transport.rideshare: Grab is the dominant ride-hailing service and is considered very safe with GPS tracking, upfront pricing, and driver verification. Both GrabCar and GrabBike (motorbike) options are available 24/7.
transport.taxis: Licensed taxi companies like Mai Linh (green cars) and Vinasun (white cars) are reliable when using the meter. Airport pickup may involve surcharges of 15,000-25,000 VND.
transport.tips: Download Grab app before arriving for seamless transportation. Always wear helmets on GrabBike rides (provided by driver). Traffic is calmer than Hanoi or Ho Chi Minh City but still requires caution when crossing streets.

customs: Remove shoes when entering homes and temples; look for shoe racks as indicators
Dress modestly in religious sites and local areas; cover shoulders and knees
Greet with 'Xin chao' and a slight bow or handshake; wait to be offered a handshake
Avoid touching someone's head or pointing feet toward people, especially in temples
Use both hands when giving or receiving business cards and gifts as a sign of respect

health.overview: Da Nang has several international-standard private hospitals and clinics that provide quality healthcare with English-speaking staff. Public hospitals are more affordable but may have language barriers and longer wait times.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Use bottled water for drinking and brushing teeth. Most restaurants and hotels provide safe bottled water.
health.vaccinations: No specific vaccinations required for entry. Consider hepatitis A & B, typhoid, and Japanese encephalitis for extended stays. Routine vaccines should be up to date.
health.altitude: Da Nang is at sea level with tropical climate. High humidity year-round. Typhoon season runs October-December with heavy rains and strong winds.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Consulate General Ho Chi Minh City: +84 28 3520 4200 (Da Nang area is covered by this office). US Embassy Hanoi: +84 24 3850 5000 for major emergencies.

faq.q: Is Da Nang safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Da Nang is considered one of Vietnam's safest cities for tourists with low crime rates, friendly locals, and strong tourism infrastructure. The US State Department maintains a Level 1 advisory recommending normal precautions.

faq.q: Is Da Nang safe at night?
faq.a: Da Nang is generally safe at night, especially in tourist areas like My Khe Beach, An Thuong, and the Han River district. These areas have good lighting, police patrols, and active nightlife until around 2 AM.

faq.q: Is Da Nang safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, Da Nang is considered one of the safest destinations in Southeast Asia for solo female travelers. Women report feeling comfortable during the day and in well-lit areas at night, with low harassment rates and respectful local culture.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Da Nang?
faq.a: There are no particularly dangerous areas, but avoid poorly lit alleyways at night and be cautious on winding mountain roads in Son Tra Peninsula. Stick to main tourist areas like My Khe Beach and An Thuong for the safest experience.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Da Nang?
faq.a: Grab ride-hailing is the safest and most convenient option. Licensed taxis are reliable when metered. Public buses are safe but may have language barriers. GrabBike (motorbike taxi) is popular and safe when wearing helmets.

cities.name: Hanoi
cities.summary: Hanoi is considered one of the safest destinations in Southeast Asia for tourists, ranking well below most major cities worldwide for crime. While petty theft and tourist scams exist, violent crime against foreigners is extremely rare, making it particularly safe for solo travelers including women.
cities.quickVerdict: Hanoi offers excellent safety for tourists with low violent crime rates and good infrastructure. The biggest concerns are petty theft (particularly motorbike bag snatching), tourist scams, and challenging traffic conditions. The US State Department gives Vietnam its lowest risk rating of Level 1, advising only normal precautions. With proper awareness and basic precautions, Hanoi provides a safe and rewarding travel experience for visitors of all backgrounds.

neighborhoods.name: Hoan Kiem District (Old Quarter)
neighborhoods.description: The tourist heart of Hanoi with heavy police presence and constant foot traffic. While petty theft can occur due to crowds, it's very safe overall with excellent emergency response.

neighborhoods.name: Ba Dinh District
neighborhoods.description: Home to government buildings and embassies, this area has exceptional security. Well-lit streets and regular police patrols make it one of the safest areas day and night.

neighborhoods.name: Dong Da District
neighborhoods.description: A predominantly residential area popular with expats. Generally safe with good lighting and regular transport links, though more quiet at night than central areas.

neighborhoods.name: Tay Ho (West Lake) District
neighborhoods.description: Upscale expat area with international restaurants and hotels. Very safe during the day, though some lakeside areas can be poorly lit at night.

neighborhoods.name: Hai Ba Trung District
neighborhoods.description: Mixed residential and commercial area that's generally safe but requires more caution at night. Some areas lack adequate street lighting and police presence.

neighborhoods.name: Long Bien District
neighborhoods.description: Industrial area across the Red River with limited tourist infrastructure. While not dangerous, it requires extra caution due to poor lighting and fewer people around after dark.

scams.name: Motorbike Bag Snatching
scams.description: Thieves on motorbikes snatch bags, phones, and cameras from pedestrians, especially in crowded tourist areas. They target tourists walking near roads or riding in cyclos.
scams.howToAvoid: Walk on inner sidewalks away from traffic, use bags with cut-proof straps, keep valuables secured across your body, and avoid displaying expensive items openly.

scams.name: Fake Photography Services
scams.description: Street vendors with fruit baskets or fake photography students approach tourists for photos, then demand payment for prints or claim you must buy the fruit they prepared during the photo.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely decline photo offers from strangers, ask for university ID if someone claims to be a student, and never agree to photos without confirming costs upfront.

scams.name: Taxi and Transport Overcharging
scams.description: Airport and tourist area taxi drivers quote inflated fixed fares or use rigged meters. Some lock passengers in to extort higher payments or take unnecessarily long routes.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official taxi stands, insist on meter use, check Grab app for fair prices, write down taxi details, and avoid taxis that approach you at airports.

scams.name: Shoe Shine Pressure Scam
scams.description: Aggressive shoe shiners approach tourists and begin cleaning shoes without permission, then demand payment 10 times higher than normal rates. Some may even remove shoes forcibly.
scams.howToAvoid: Keep walking when approached about shoes, firmly say no without stopping, avoid areas where you see multiple shoe shiners operating, and wear shoes in good condition.

soloFemale.overview: Hanoi is exceptionally safe for solo female travelers, ranking among the top destinations in Southeast Asia for women traveling alone. Vietnamese culture respects women, violent crime against tourists is extremely rare, and the city has good infrastructure for independent travel. Local women are empowered and often run businesses, creating a supportive environment for female visitors.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed hostels in the Old Quarter or Ba Dinh areas with female-only dorms for safety and social opportunities
Use reputable taxi companies like Mai Linh or Grab app rather than street taxis, especially from airports and tourist areas
Dress modestly when visiting temples or rural areas by covering shoulders and knees to show cultural respect
Be aware of motorbike bag snatchers by keeping bags across your body and walking away from traffic on inner sidewalks
Trust your instincts and politely decline overly friendly strangers who approach you in tourist areas

nightSafety.overview: Hanoi is generally safe at night, especially in tourist areas like the Old Quarter and around Hoan Kiem Lake where activity continues late. Street lighting is good in central areas, and police presence is visible. However, some residential areas and outer districts have poor lighting and fewer people around after 11 PM.
tips: Stick to well-lit main streets and tourist areas like the Old Quarter when walking at night
Use Grab or reputable taxi companies rather than walking long distances in unfamiliar areas after dark
Avoid excessive alcohol consumption and stay with groups when visiting bars or night markets
Keep emergency numbers saved and ensure your phone is charged for transportation apps and communication
Be extra cautious of motorbike traffic at night when visibility is reduced and some drivers may have been drinking

transport.metro: Hanoi's metro system is modern, clean, and very safe with security cameras and regular police presence. Line 2A connects central areas and is reliable for tourists. Stations have English signage and announcements.
transport.rideshare: Grab is widely available, safe, and reliable with fixed pricing and driver tracking. Much safer than street taxis and helps avoid overcharging scams. Drivers are generally professional and vehicles are in good condition.
transport.taxis: Use only reputable companies like Mai Linh, Vinasun, or Hanoi Taxi Group. Avoid taxis that approach you at airports or tourist sites. Insist on meter use and check routes on your phone to prevent overcharging.
transport.tips: Avoid xe om (motorcycle taxis) unless through official apps, as unlicensed drivers pose safety risks. The US State Department specifically warns against using unregulated motorcycle taxis due to poor safety equipment and unscrupulous operators.

customs: Remove shoes when entering homes, temples, and some businesses - look for shoe racks or other shoes outside as indicators
Show respect to elders by greeting them first and using appropriate titles like 'Ong' (sir) and 'Ba' (madam)
Use both hands when giving or receiving items like business cards, money, or gifts to show respect
Dress modestly in temples and rural areas by covering shoulders and knees - carry a light scarf for temple visits
Avoid pointing with your feet or index finger - use your whole hand to gesture toward people or objects
Keep public displays of affection minimal as Vietnamese culture values modesty in relationships

health.overview: Healthcare quality in Hanoi varies significantly between public and private facilities. Private international hospitals offer Western-standard care with English-speaking staff, while public hospitals may lack resources and language support. Most common health issues for tourists include respiratory problems from air pollution and gastrointestinal issues from food/water.
health.water: Tap water is not safe for drinking. Use bottled water for drinking and brushing teeth. Avoid ice in drinks unless at reputable establishments. Be cautious with fresh fruits and vegetables that may have been washed with tap water.
health.vaccinations: No vaccinations are required for entry, but hepatitis A and B are recommended. Japanese encephalitis is only recommended for extended rural stays. Ensure routine vaccinations like measles and tetanus are up to date.
health.altitude: Hanoi is at sea level with a humid subtropical climate. Air pollution is a major concern, especially for those with respiratory conditions. Monitor air quality apps and limit outdoor activities during high pollution days.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Hanoi: 7 Lang Ha Street, Ba Dinh District, Tel: +84 24 3850 5000. Emergency American Citizen Services available 24/7.

faq.q: Is Hanoi safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Hanoi is very safe for tourists. It has low violent crime rates and ranks among the safest cities in Southeast Asia. The US State Department gives Vietnam its lowest risk Level 1 rating. Main concerns are petty theft and tourist scams, which can be avoided with basic precautions.

faq.q: Is Hanoi safe at night?
faq.a: Hanoi is generally safe at night, especially in tourist areas like the Old Quarter which remain active late. Central areas have good lighting and police presence. However, avoid poorly lit outer districts and excessive drinking, and use official transport rather than walking long distances after dark.

faq.q: Is Hanoi safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, Hanoi is excellent for solo female travelers and ranks as one of the safest destinations in Southeast Asia for women. Vietnamese culture respects women, violent crime is extremely rare, and the infrastructure supports independent travel. Use common sense precautions and stay in tourist areas.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Hanoi?
faq.a: There are no outright dangerous areas, but exercise more caution in outer districts like Long Bien at night due to poor lighting and fewer people. Some parts of Hai Ba Trung can be quiet after dark. Stick to central tourist areas like Old Quarter and Ba Dinh for maximum safety.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Hanoi?
faq.a: Yes, public transport is generally safe. The metro is modern and secure with good security measures. Use Grab app for rideshare services rather than street taxis. Avoid unlicensed motorcycle taxis (xe om) as they lack proper safety equipment and regulation.

cities.name: Ho Chi Minh City
cities.summary: Ho Chi Minh City is generally safe for tourists with very low violent crime rates against foreigners, though petty theft and motorbike snatching are common concerns. The US State Department maintains a Level 1 'Exercise Normal Precautions' advisory for Vietnam. While traffic chaos and occasional scams require vigilance, the city ranks among Southeast Asia's safer destinations with good tourism infrastructure and helpful locals.
cities.quickVerdict: Ho Chi Minh City is a vibrant, largely safe destination where violent crime against tourists is extremely rare. The main safety concerns are opportunistic theft (especially phone snatching by motorbike), traffic navigation, and tourist-targeted scams. With basic street smarts and awareness of common scams like rigged taxi meters and overpriced street vendors, visitors can explore confidently. The city has solid healthcare infrastructure, helpful locals, and strong police presence in tourist areas.

neighborhoods.name: District 1 (Ben Thanh Ward)
neighborhoods.description: The main tourist district with high police presence, well-lit streets, and numerous hotels. Close to major attractions like Independence Palace and War Remnants Museum. Very walkable with good infrastructure and plenty of legitimate taxis and Grab pickups.

neighborhoods.name: District 3 (Ben Nghe Ward)
neighborhoods.description: Central area with mix of business and residential, generally safe for tourists. Good restaurant scene and cultural sites. Well-connected to other districts with reliable transport options and decent nighttime lighting.

neighborhoods.name: District 7 (Phu My Hung)
neighborhoods.description: Modern planned district popular with expats and tourists. Clean, well-developed area with international hospitals, shopping malls, and upscale accommodation. Slightly removed from city center but very safe day and night.

neighborhoods.name: District 4
neighborhoods.description: Working-class area that can be rough after dark. Higher crime rates and fewer tourist amenities. While not dangerous during the day, solo travelers should exercise extra caution at night and avoid poorly lit areas.

neighborhoods.name: District 8
neighborhoods.description: One of the poorest districts frequently affected by flooding. Higher petty crime rates and limited tourist infrastructure. During flood season, regular inundation makes navigation difficult and increases safety risks.

neighborhoods.name: Tan Son Nhat Airport Area
neighborhoods.description: Generally safe transit area but watch for fake taxi drivers and airport scams. Stick to official taxi companies like Mai Linh or Vinasun, or use Grab app. Late night arrivals should pre-book accommodation and transport.

scams.name: Motorbike Phone Snatching
scams.description: Thieves on motorbikes snatch phones from pedestrians near traffic areas, especially in District 1 around Ben Thanh Market and busy streets.
scams.howToAvoid: Hold phones securely away from street side, use crossbody bags, avoid using phones near curbs. When taking photos, step away from traffic.

scams.name: Rigged Taxi Meters
scams.description: Unscrupulous taxi drivers use modified meters that run fast or quote prices in USD instead of VND. Some fake taxi companies impersonate legitimate ones.
scams.howToAvoid: Use only Mai Linh, Vinasun, or Grab app. Check meter is running and starts at correct rate. Agree on currency (VND) upfront.

scams.name: Cyclo Overcharging
scams.description: Pedicab drivers quote low prices but demand much higher payment at the end, claiming price was per person or adding 'waiting time' fees.
scams.howToAvoid: Negotiate total price clearly before starting. Clarify if price is per person or group. Book through reputable operators rather than street touts.

scams.name: Street Vendor Photo Scam
scams.description: Coconut sellers and street vendors offer free photos with props then demand payment for the 'experience' or opened coconuts.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely refuse unsolicited photo offers. If you want coconut, negotiate price beforehand and be clear about what you're purchasing.

soloFemale.overview: Ho Chi Minh City is relatively safe for solo female travelers with many women reporting positive experiences. The main concerns are petty theft and navigating chaotic traffic rather than harassment or assault.
